title: The Vanished Fair
passage: The account books of the small Fenland town of Nettleholt are unusually complete for the seventeenth century, which is what makes one absence so striking. Every autumn from 1610 onward the borough chamberlain sets down the takings of the Michaelmas goose fair — the rents from the stalls, the fines levied on unlicensed traders, the few shillings paid to the town waits who played there. Then, after the autumn of 1631, the fair vanishes from the ledgers altogether. No line records its cancellation; the same meticulous chamberlain, who noted even the cost of mending a single lock, sets down nothing at all. The pages for the following Michaelmas are given over entirely to work on the river wall, which that season devoured a sum far larger than the fair had ever yielded — and which was raised, the entries show, on the very meadow where the stalls had once stood.

questions.prompt: What does the text suggest about why the goose fair disappears from Nettleholt's ledgers after 1631?

options.text: The chamberlain simply stopped recording its takings, though the fair itself carried on as before.
Michaelmas goose fairs were being abandoned across England throughout these decades.
The fair was formally abolished by an order that the chamberlain chose not to enter in the books.
The costly river-wall works had taken over both the town's money and the meadow the fair needed, so it lapsed.

questions.rationale: TYPE-002 inference, one inch beyond the text. The passage supplies the pieces — the fair drops out after 1631, no cancellation is recorded, and the next Michaelmas pages go entirely to a river wall that cost more than the fair earned and was built 'on the very meadow where the stalls had once stood' — but never states why. D supplies the single missing link. A = too_literal: it restates the silence but is contradicted by the meadow being built over ('carried on as before' cannot hold once the site is gone). B = outside_knowledge / two-step leap: an England-wide trend the passage never asserts. C = contradiction: the text says 'No line records its cancellation', so inventing a deliberately-omitted formal order is unsupported and against the text.

generator_meta.originality_note: Nettleholt, its ledgers, the fair and the river-wall episode are all invented. 'Town waits' (municipal musicians) and the Michaelmas goose-fair custom are generic period colour, not a real event; no real person is named or quoted.
